message:
perf(fj-dispatch): native F32 fast path for scalar scan/while batching (frankenjax-l3zw)

BatchTrace scalar scan/while fast paths previously only matched
(I64, I64) and (F64, F64) literal pairs in apply_scan_scalar_op,
apply_while_scalar_op, and apply_while_scalar_cond — every other
combination fell through to None, forcing native F32Bits scalar
lanes onto the generic per-element control-flow batching path.
That left a measurable parity gap behind for f32 carries, which the
just-landed dispatch_baseline benches (frankenjax-l3zw) made
visible.

Add the missing (F32Bits, F32Bits) arm in all three sites with the
same shape as the F64 arms:

apply_scan_scalar_op
- Decode both bits with f32::from_bits, dispatch through the existing
  ScanScalarOp enum (Add/Sub/Mul/Div/Pow/Max/Min), re-encode via
  Literal::from_f32 so the literal width stays at F32Bits — no
  silent F32→F64 widening that would change downstream dtype
  inference.

apply_while_scalar_op
- Same pattern across WhileScalarOp (Add/Sub/ReverseSub/Mul/Div/
  ReverseDiv/Pow/ReversePow), preserving F32 literal width.

apply_while_scalar_cond
- Six predicate ops (Lt/Le/Gt/Ge/Ne/Eq) on F32Bits operands. NaN
  semantics ride on rust's f32 comparison defaults, matching the
  existing F64 arm exactly.

Add four test helpers (make_f32_vector, make_f32_matrix,
extract_f32_vec, assert_f32_close) and two regression tests:
- test_batch_trace_scan_batched_xs_f32_add_preserves_literal_width
- test_batch_trace_while_batched_f32_add_preserves_literal_width

Both tests assert that the fast-path output retains DType::F32
rather than being upcast — that's the user-visible invariant the
generic fallback doesn't guarantee.

The vmap_tensor_ops oracle/e2e artifacts pick up only timestamp
deltas (no record/case/scenario diffs), confirming the new arm
produces parity output and is safe to ship together with the
implementation. frankenjax-l3zw stays in_progress — benchmark
before/after evidence is the next step.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
